Results: Fifteen patients were enrolled (median age 44, range 35–83 years); one patient experienced a non-vaccine-related adverse event after one vaccination and withdrew. Treatment-related adverse events (n = 8) were grade 1, primarily at the injection site, and self-resolved. No recurrence was observed. TA-CIN-specific antibody titers tended to be higher in thigh-vaccinated patients. Bulk TCRseq revealed significant increases in expanded and de novo T cell clones following thigh-vaccination compared with the arm. No correlation with prior treatment modality was observed. E6-, E7-, and L2-specific TCR clones expanded, although L2-specific T cell responses were predominant. One month post-vaccination, scRNAseq revealed significant expansion of MAIT and cytotoxic CD8+ T cells, and both expanded and novel TCR clonotypes were identified in the latter. Conclusions: Thigh or arm vaccination with TA-CIN was well tolerated, but the former elicited higher CD8 T cell and antibody responses in HPV16+ cervical cancer patients with NED after primary therapy.

title: Bao Yuan Gao vs. Calendula cream for radiotherapy-induced skin toxicity in head and neck cancer: A randomized controlled trial – A biased negative trial essentially
authors: Wong H.C.Y., Karam I., Corbin K., Tse S.S.W., Hircock C., Chow E. et al.

title: Nal-IRI/LV5-FU versus paclitaxel as second-line therapy in patients with metastatic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (PRODIGE 62-FFCD 1701-OESIRI)
authors: Tougeron D., Mineur L., Zaanan A., Kadi M., Poisson Ligeza C., Bourgeois V. et al.
abstract: Background: Metastatic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (mESCC) patients with disease progression after platinum-based first-line chemotherapy (CT) +/- immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) may benefit from second-line CT, mostly based on paclitaxel and irinotecan, but no randomized trial has compared these regimens. Patients and methods: PRODIGE 62-OESIRI is a multicenter, open-label, randomized phase II trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of nanoliposomal irinotecan (Nal-IRI) plus 5FU versus paclitaxel as second-line CT in mESCC. The primary endpoint was to achieve overall survival (OS) of 60 % at 9 months. Results: Between March 2019 and July 2023, 106 pts were randomized. Median age was 65.6 years, 83.0 % men and 29.2 %/58.5 %/12.3 % of ECOG PS 0/1/2, respectively. As prior treatment, 48.6 % had chemoradiation, 41.9 % CT alone and 9.5 % CT plus ICI. OS at 9 months was 34.0 % [90 %CI: 22.9–46.5] and 39.2 % [90 %CI: 27.7–51.7] in the 5FU Nal-IRI and paclitaxel arms, respectively. The primary endpoint was not met. Median progression-free survival was 2.4 [95 %CI: 2.1–3.6] and 2.1 [95 %CI: 1.9–3.3] months, and median OS 7.1 [95 %CI: 5.2–8.3] and 6.6 [95 %CI: 4.8–10.3] months, respectively. Overall, 51.0 % and 38.5 % of patients experienced at least one grade 3–4 treatment-related adverse events (neuropathy: 2.0 vs. 7.7 %, diarrhea: 16.3 % vs. 0 % and vomiting: 10.2 vs. 0 %), in the 5FU Nal-IRI and paclitaxel arms, respectively. Treatment was discontinued for toxicity in 10.4 % versus 3.9 % in the 5FU Nal-IRI and paclitaxel arm, respectively. Conclusions: PRODIGE 62-OESIRI trial showed low efficacy of paclitaxel and 5FU Nal-IRI in the 2nd line treatment of mESCC, though paclitaxel provided a better safety profile. Trial registration NCT03719924

title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors in Merkel Cell Carcinoma of the Skin: A 2025 Comprehensive Review
authors: Tai P., Alqaisi O., Al-Ghabeesh S., Sijarina L., Yu E., Thachuthara A.J. et al.
abstract: Objective: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare and aggressive form of skin cancer. Although immunotherapy has transformed MCC management, published data remain limited. This comprehensive review evaluates current evidence on immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) in MCC, in relation to other treatment modalities such as surgery and radiotherapy. Methods: Peer-reviewed articles published between January 2000 and August 2025 were searched manually in four databases: Scopus, ScienceDirect, PubMed and MEDLINE, using the keywords “Merkel cell carcinoma” AND “immunotherapy” AND “immune checkpoint inhibitors”. The Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) methodology was employed. Results: ICIs can be given in different settings: (A) Neoadjuvant: The CheckMate 358 trial reported a 54.5% response rate among 33 radiologically evaluable patients treated with nivolumab, each showing over 30% tumor reduction. (B) Adjuvant: (1) The ADMEC-O phase II trial demonstrated improved disease-free survival with adjuvant nivolumab. (2) The ADAM phase III trial evaluates adjuvant avelumab in node-positive patients post-surgery/radiation, with common side effects including nausea, fatigue, and itching. (3) STAMP, a phase III trial, investigates pembrolizumab in stage I–III MCC. Both ADAM and STAMP have completed accrual and results are pending. (C) Primary therapy: KEYNOTE-017 and JAVELIN trials reported a 60% overall response rate and ~40% 3-year progression-free survival with first-line pembrolizumab or avelumab. Both agents also show promise as salvage therapies. Conclusions: ICIs demonstrate encouraging outcomes in MCC across various treatment stages. Continued research is essential to optimize treatment timing and integrate multimodal therapies.

title: A systematic review of the applications of the real option value of medical technologies in oncology
authors: Adjei K., Diaby V., Li M., Sherbeny F., Suther S., Ali A.A.
journal: Journal of Managed Care and Specialty Pharmacy
abstract: BACKGROUND: Real option value (ROV) offers an innovative paradigm to evaluate the dynamic value of medical technologies, particularly in cancer, by capturing the value of extending patient survival to access future innovations. Despite its potential, the application of ROV in medical technologies in oncology remains underexplored. OBJECTIVE: To synthesize existing evidence on the application of ROV in medical technologies in oncology. METHODS: A comprehensive search of PubMed, ScienceDirect, and Web of Science was conducted to identify peer-reviewed studies published in English from January 2000 to May 2024. In the search query, a combination of keywords related to “real option value” and “cancer” was used. Key data extracted included study characteristics, objectives, ROV modeling technique, and primary findings. The Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards 2022 checklist was used for quality assessment of the studies. RESULTS: A total of 13 of 165 studies assessed the ROV of medical therapies, with a primary focus on melanoma, lung cancer, and prostate cancer. ROV was modeled from the ex post and ex ante perspectives. The methodologies employed vary, with common forecasting approaches including the Lee-Carter model to project future decreases in mortality rates, fitting Cox proportional regression models on administrative claims data, or estimating the approval likelihood of early pipeline drugs based on data from early randomized clinical trials. CONCLUSIONS: The ROV represents a critical dimension in evaluating medical technologies in oncology, where innovation is rapid. The implications of ROV extend beyond oncology, with the potential to influence funding, pricing, and access decisions in other disease areas as well. However, challenges such as oversimplification of assumptions for forecasting, methodological consistency, and lack of standardized framework remain pervasive. This systematic review underscores the need to integrate ROV into Health Technology Assessment practices to inform resource allocation and policy decisions.

title: Neoadjuvant (177)Lutetium-PSMA-617 Radioligand Therapy for High-Risk Localized Prostate Cancer: Rationale, Early Clinical Evidence, and Future Directions
authors: Kwon W.-A., Joung J.Y.
abstract: Men with high-risk localized prostate cancer (PCa) often have poor long-term outcomes, underscoring the need for improved neoadjuvant strategies beyond the current standard of care. Radioligand therapy with (177)Lutetium-PSMA-617 ((177)Lu-PSMA-617) has emerged as a promising method to eliminate occult micrometastases while enhancing immune-mediated clearance of the primary tumor. Initial trials have affirmed the treatment’s feasibility and safety; however, they have consistently reported a lack of pathological complete response. This absence of profound initial tumor reduction necessitates further therapeutic advancements. The underlying rationale for future strategies is clear, as (177)Lu-PSMA-617 promotes immunogenic cell death, potentially sensitizing immunologically “cold” tumors to checkpoint inhibitors. However, caution is warranted. The synergy observed between these therapies in advanced, metastatic castration-resistant PCa stems from a different biological context, and similar outcomes cannot be presumed in treatment-naïve, localized disease without rigorous validation. Continued progress hinges on developing improved metrics for success and patient selection. Simple prostate-specific antigen reductions have demonstrated minimal correlation with significant pathological outcomes in this setting, underscoring the critical need for validated surrogate endpoints and predictive biomarkers. Ultimately, large-scale randomized trials are essential to determine whether this investigational approach impacts key clinical outcomes—namely, metastasis-free and overall survival. While the strategy is theoretically sound, its capacity to enhance cure rates for high-risk localized PCa remains unverified.

title: P17.24.B BEVACIZUMAB VERSUS DEXAMETHASONE AS FIRSTLINE TREATMENT FOR SYMPTOMATIC CEREBRAL RADIATION NECROSIS AFTER RADIATION OF HIGH GRADE GLIOMA OR BRAIN METASTASES: A RANDOMIZED CLINICAL TRIAL
authors: Kroon L.L., Dieleman E.M., Hassan S., Keil V.C., Van Barreveld M., Dijkgraaf M.G. et al.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Cerebral radiation necrosis (CRN) is a serious late complication of high-dose radiation therapy for brain metastases (BM) or gliomas, potentially leading to significant neurological symptoms and impaired quality of life (QoL). First-line therapy with corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) frequently causes side effects, may lead to refractory neurological symptoms in some cases, and can interfere with immunotherapy. Bevacizumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) has shown promise as a second-line treatment for steroidrefractory sCRN. However, the optimal first-line treatment for symptomatic CRN (sCRN) remains unclear. MATERIAL AND METHODS: The BRAINS study is a multicenter, randomized clinical trial evaluating the clinical efficacy and cost-effectiveness of bevacizumab versus dexamethasone as first-line therapy for sCRN in two parallel patient cohorts: those with high-grade glioma (HGG) and BM. Eligible patients are adults with sCRN ≥ 3 months post-stereotactic radiotherapy of BM or focal radiotherapy for HGG, a Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS) score ≤ 90, and a decline of ≥2 points in at least one domain of the Neurologic Assessment in Neuro- Oncology (NANO) scale. Patients who have received prior treatment with bevacizumab within the last 6 months, require decompressive surgery, have any comorbidity that contraindicates study medication, or have a life expectancy of less than 3 months are ineligible. A total of 408 patients will be randomized in a 1: 1 ratio to receive either: 1) intravenous bevacizumab (flat dose 600 mg every three weeks for four cycles), or 2) daily oral dexamethasone, tapered over 12 weeks. Dexamethasone may be used concurrently with bevacizumab if clinically indicated. Follow-up of patients will be 2 years. The primary endpoint is a favorable clinical response at 12 weeks, defined as dexamethasone use of ≤1.5mg/day combined with either ≥ 10 points improvement in KPS with at least stable NANO or ≥ 2 points improvement in NANO with at least stable KPS. Key secondary endpoints are: QoL, cognitive function, epileptic seizure control, toxicity, recurrence of sCRN, and cost-effectiveness. The first patients will be enrolled from May 2025 onwards. Patient recruitment is expected to be completed by May 2028. The BRAINS study is funded by the Dutch Cancer Society (project no. 14642).

title: OS04.6.A MULTICENTER RANDOMIZED PHASE II STUDY OF R-MPV-A CHEMOIMMUNOTHERAPY WITH OR WITHOUT LOWDOSE WHOLE-BRAIN RADIOTHERAPY FOR NEWLY DIAGNOSED PRIMARY CNS LYMPHOMA: NRG/ RTOG 1114
authors: Omuro A., Deangelis L., Polley M.C., Correa D., Wefel J.S., Bovi J.A. et al.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Methotrexate-based chemoradiotherapy is effective in primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL), but carries a risk of severe neurotoxicity. In a single-arm study, a chemotherapy regimen with methotrexate, procarbazine, vincristine and cytarabine was combined with rituximab (R-MPV-A) and substantially reduced doses of whole-brain radiotherapy (LD-WBRT), resulting in excellent progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). Because R-MPV-A had never been tested without radiation, we conducted a randomized study to determine if this low radiotherapy dose was relevant for disease control, and to characterize the neurotoxicity of this approach. MATERIAL AND METHODS: Patients were randomized to receive R-MPV-A alone (Chemo arm) or combined with LD-WBRT (ChemoRT arm), given at 2,340 cGy (180cGy X13). Methotrexate dose was 3.5g/m2 in 2-hour infusions. Primary endpoint was intent-to-treat (ITT) PFS. A sample size of 89 would provide 80% power to detect a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.63 (one-sided alpha=0.15). The study was supported by grants U10CA180868 (NRG Oncology Operations) and U10CA180822 (NRG Oncology SDMC) from the National Cancer Institute (NCI). RESULTS: A total of 91 patients were randomized, and 44 were analyzed in the ChemoRT arm and 46 in the Chemo arm. Median age was 66 and 59.5, respectively. R-MPV-A was well tolerated with high overall response rate (ORR) in both arms; radiographic complete response rate was 92.3% (ChemoRT) and 76.3% (Chemo). After median follow-up of 4.6 years, median PFS was not reached (ChemoRT) vs 2.1 years (Chemo), HR=0.47 (p=0.007). The 2-year PFS was 78.7% vs 54%, respectively. OS favored ChemoRT but differences were not statistically significant (HR 0.71; p=0.33). Comprehensive neuropsychological evaluation showed no differences in cognitive outcomes, with several tests favoring ChemoRT, possibly reflecting more relapses and salvage treatments in the Chemo arm. CONCLUSION: The study met the primary endpoint, demonstrating the addition of LD-WBRT to chemoimmunotherapy with R-MPV-A significantly improves PFS in newly-diagnosed PCNSL, with favorable neurocognitive outcomes.

title: USING A LARGE LANGUAGE MODEL TO ASSESS DATA IN PUBMED ABSTRACTS
authors: Burke H., Gagarin V., Hoang A.
abstract: BACKGROUND: PubMed stores and indexes medical abstracts and is the largest biomedical database in the world; consisting of 24,358,442 records and containing 122 GB of data. We are interested in using the knowledge in PubMed to advance medical research and the practice of medicine. To achieve this goal, we enlisted a large language model (LLM). LLMs can input and output natural language meanings and they can make inferences based on meanings. We hypothesize that an LLM can detect medical information, categorize and tabulate frequencies, and associate and summarize the medical information contained in PubMed abstracts. Our target condition is ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS). METHODS: We investigated changes in the types, frequencies, and efficacy of DCIS treatments and the role biomarkers played in directing treatment in papers published from 2014 through 2023. We used the key words ductal carcinoma in situ or DCIS and randomized clinical trial. We report the comparison of the two-year time intervals 2014-2015 and 2022-2023. We used python to clean and format the abstracts, we created prompts that included the abstracts, we entered the prompts into the LLM Gemini Pro 1.5, and we assessed the output and refined the prompts to generate the final LLM results. The treatments were: surgery alone, radiotherapy alone, endocrine therapy alone, surgery and radiotherapy, surgery and endocrine therapy, combination endocrine therapy, vaccine therapy, and active surveillance. The results were checked by a human rater. RESULTS: Comparing the two time intervals, the LLM found that as a percentage of all treatments in each time interval; surgical treatment increased from 11% to 36%, radiation decreased from 30% to 14%, endocrine therapy remained approximately the same at 15% to 14%, active surveillance increased from 7% to 14%, surgery plus radiation decreased from 30% to 9%, surgery plus endocrine remained approximately the same at 7% to 9%, vaccine therapy increased from 0 to 4%, dietary modification occurred in 2 trials, and vitamin/mineral supplementation occurred in 2 trials. In terms of biomarker-directed treatment, the LLM found that DCIS patients positive for erbb-2 benefited from radiotherapy and those positive for PDGFRb did not benefit from radiotherapy, that ER positivity predicted benefit from tamoxifen and aromatase inhibitors whereas COX-2 positivity predicted resistance to aromatase inhibitors, and that erbb-2 positive patients responded to vaccine treatment. The results were checked and they were correct. CONCLUSIONS: These findings suggest a shift in clinical trials toward surgical interventions for DCIS and a away from studies investigating radiotherapy, either alone or in combination with surgery. Large language models are a powerful tool for mining PubMed. Not only can they reliably find information in abstracts, they can tabulate frequencies, and they can associate and summarize information within and across abstracts.

title: Efficacy and hepatotoxicity of tumor treatment with immune checkpoint inhibitors-a meta-analysis
authors: Dian W., Dan G.Y., Jing L., Bo Z.X., Yi L.S., Yu L.X.
abstract: Background: The rising incidence of malignant tumors, coupled with the strong adverse reactions associated with traditional radiotherapy and chemotherapy, prompted a pivotal moment in the emergence of tumor immune therapy, notably represented by immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs). Currently, the main target molecules of ICIs include cytotoxic T lymphocyte-related molecule 4 (CTLA-4), programmed cell death receptor-1 (PD-1), programmed cell death ligand-1 (PDL1), and the third immune checkpoint, lymphocyte activation gene 3 (LAG3, CD223). However, the benefits of ICIs are concurrent with immune-related adverse events (irAEs), disrupting the delicate balance of immune tolerance. While irAEs can affect any organ system, the liver emerges as the most common target. Studies indicate that the incidence of IMH depends on factors such as type, dosage, and monotherapy vs combination therapy. When used alone, the incidence of any level of IMH is typically approximately 10%. Although most cases are mild and self-resolving, clinical trials and post-marketing monitoring reveal that severe cases may result in fatal acute liver failure. The World Health Organization (WHO) pharmacovigilance database (Vigilyze) reports that 20.2% of patients (124/613 cases) succumb to ICIs-related hepatotoxicity in fatal adverse events. Method: A systematic search of PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library, spanning from their inception to June 2022, was conducted. Full articles were further assessed for indications of being randomized clinical trials for ICI therapy. The primary outcomes were progression-free survival and IMH. Hazard Ratios for PFS and Odds Ratios for IMH were calculated using Review Manager 5.3 and STATA 16 software. Result: A total of 15 articles were finally included. Compared to the control group, ICI therapy was significantly associated with improved PFS in three non-small cell lung cancer studies (HR, 0.53; 95% CI, 0.46-0.61; p<0.001) and three melanoma studies (HR, 0.70; 95% CI, 0.58-0.84; p<0.001). The overall incidence of IMH in patients with tumors treated with ICIs was 9.47% (OR, 2.30; 95% CI, 1.66-3.19; p<0.05). Additionally, 13 articles conducted subgroup analyses based on tumor type, revealing a 12.97% incidence of anygrade IMH in the melanoma group (OR, 3.60; 95% CI, 2.71-4.76; p<0.05). Conclusion: For melanoma and NSCLC, ICIs exhibit superior efficacy in PFS compared to the control group, resulting in an overall reduction in the relative risk of death. However, the literature consistently indicated a significant hepatotoxic profile for ICIs, with higher rates of grade 3 or 4 IMH. Patients with melanoma demonstrated a higher incidence of any-grade IMH in different tumor types.

title: Atezolizumab +/- Tiragolumab Before and After Chemoradiation for Unresectable Stage III Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (AFT-57)
authors: Ross H.J., Kozono D.E., Mix M.D., Urbanic J.J., Williams T.M., O'Donnell T. et al.
abstract: Introduction: Some patients with unresectable stage III non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) may be cured by chemoradiotherapy (CRT) and adjuvant checkpoint inhibition through PD-L1 blockade. Durvalumab improved survival compared to placebo in the PACIFIC trial (median progression-free survival (mPFS) 16.9 vs 5.6 months (mo), median overall survival (mOS) 47.5 vs 29.1 mo compared to CRT alone) (Spigel D, et al J Clin Oncol 2022). Five-year survival remained below 50% and only patients completing CRT without progression and with good performance status (PS) were eligible to receive adjuvant immunotherapy. Neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibition allows more patients the potential benefit of immunotherapy and may attenuate tumor-related immunosuppression via depletion of regulatory T cells and clonal expansion of effector T cells, thereby improving tumor immunogenicity. Neoadjuvant atezolizumab appeared promising compared to historic PACIFIC data in the phase II single-arm AFT-16 trial (mPFS 30 mo and mOS not reached; Ross HJ et al JAMA Oncology 2024). Tiragolumab blocks the immunosuppressive protein TIGIT, and may decrease aspects of the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment through effects on both lymphoid and myeloid cells. Whether combination immunotherapy with atezolizumab and tiragolumab before and after CRT will further improve outcomes in this setting is the question posed in the AFT-57 trial. Methods: This phase II randomized Alliance Foundation Trials study (AFT-57) evaluates safety and efficacy of atezolizumab with or without the anti-TIGIT monoclonal antibody tiragolumab before and after definitive CRT. The primary endpoint is PFS in the two experimental arms to select the most promising regimen for further study. AFT-57 will randomly assign 158 patients with unresectable stage III NSCLC (AJCC v8), Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group PS 0-1, no active autoimmune disease and no significant underlying organ dysfunction to 2 cycles of neoadjuvant atezolizumab (intravenous [IV] 1200 mg every 21 days or subcutaneous 1875 mg every 21 days) with or without tiragolumab (600 mg IV). Non-progressing patients undergo CRT with carboplatin and paclitaxel weekly concurrent with 60 Gray involved-field thoracic radiation in 30 daily fractions delivered Monday to Friday over 6 weeks followed by atezolizumab with or without tiragolumab every 21 days on the same schedule as the neoadjuvant doses, with a plan for one year of therapy absent progression or intolerance. Correlative science includes tissue and blood-based immune-related biomarkers at baseline and during therapy. This trial utilizes the Alliance Participant Engagement Portal, a patient-facing site designed to provide information and resources to Alliance clinical trial participants. The trial was activated on 12/7/23 and the first patient was screened 1/19/24. Clinicaltrials.gov Identifier: NCT05798663. Support: Alliance Foundation Trials, Genentech; https://acknowledgments.alliancefound.org.

title: A Phase II Trial of Induction Camrelizumab Plus Chemotherapy Followed by Chemoradiotherapy and Consolidation in Limited-Stage SCLC
authors: Liu D., Hu M., Yang L., Ni J., Xu Y.
abstract: Introduction: Limited-stage small cell lung cancer (LS-SCLC) comprises 15-30% of SCLC cases, yet relapses occur frequently following definitive therapy. Although durvalumab consolidation has enhanced outcomes compared to traditional concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CRT), disease progression remains a formidable challenge. Building on findings in locally advanced non-small cell lung cancer, where induction immunotherapy plus chemotherapy improved survival, we conducted a Phase II randomized trial to evaluate whether a similar approach might benefit patients with LS-SCLC. This trial examined the efficacy and safety of induction camrelizumab plus chemotherapy, followed by CRT and camrelizumab consolidation. Methods: This investigator-initiated, phase II, open-label, randomized controlled trial (ChiCTR2000032275) was conducted at Shanghai Pulmonary Hospital. Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to either an experimental regimen of induction immunotherapy and chemotherapy (two cycles of camrelizumab plus etoposide-platinum [EP] or etoposide-carboplatin [EC]) followed by CRT, prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI), and up to 12 months of camrelizumab consolidation (camrelizumab group), or a control regimen of induction chemotherapy (EP or EC) followed by CRT and PCI (cCRT group). The primary endpoint was the 1-year progression-free survival (PFS) rate. Secondary endpoints included overall survival (OS), objective response rate, and safety. Adverse events were assessed according to Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE) version 5.0. Kaplan-Meier estimations, stratified log-rank tests, and Cox proportional-hazards models were employed to compare survival outcomes. Results: Between November 2020 and March 2024, 65 patients were screened for eligibility, and 41 were randomly assigned to receive either camrelizumab (n=21) or standard cCRT (n=20). The median follow-up time was 20.9 months (95% CI: 18.6-23.2 months) for the camrelizumab group and 23.2 months (95% CI: 16.3-30.2 months) for the cCRT group. All patients treated with camrelizumab achieved a partial response, compared with 84.2% in the cCRT arm. The 1-year PFS rate was higher in the camrelizumab group than in the cCRT group [59.9% (95% CI: 34.6-85.2%) vs. 39.5% (95% CI: 15.6-63.4%)], and the median PFS was not reached in the camrelizumab arm, whereas it was 11.4 months (95% CI: 9.8-13.0 months) in the cCRT arm (HR 0.42, 95% CI: 0.18-0.96, p=0.047). Similarly, the median OS was not reached in the camrelizumab group, compared with 23.9 months (95% CI: 15.3-32.5) in the cCRT group (HR 0.32, 95% CI: 0.11-0.96, p=0.044). The 1-year OS rate was 100% for camrelizumab and 89.2% for cCRT. Subgroup analysis further indicated that an absolute lymphocyte count (ALC) >2.0 × 10 9/L correlated with significantly longer PFS in the camrelizumab arm. No immune-related deaths were reported; however, one patient experienced grade 3 reactive capillary endothelial proliferation. Notably, induction camrelizumab did not lead to an increased incidence of radiation pneumonitis. Conclusions: These findings suggest that induction camrelizumab plus chemotherapy followed by CRT and camrelizumab consolidation may improve both PFS and OS in LS-SCLC without exacerbating toxicity. Further research to refine treatment sequencing could provide vital improvements in managing this challenging disease.

title: Induction Immunochemotherapy Followed by Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy for Limited-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer
authors: Yang L., Liu D., Ren S., Ni J., Xu Y.
abstract: Introduction: Immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy all play significant roles in the management of small cell lung cancer (SCLC). Combining radiotherapy with chemotherapy followed by immunotherapy shows promise for limited-stage SCLC (LS-SCLC), offering good tolerability and survival benefits. However, there is limited data on whether immunotherapy combined with chemotherapy for LS-SCLC can be performed before chemoradiotherapy. Therefore, we investigated the safety and effectiveness of induction immunochemotherapy followed by concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CCRT) in patients with LS-SCLC, aiming to contribute new insights to the treatment of SCLC. Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 138 patients with LS-SCLC who received chemoradiotherapy between January 2019 and January 2025. This study included 34 patients receiving induction immunochemotherapy and 104 patients receiving induction chemotherapy. All patients underwent 2-4 cycles of induction therapy before CCRT. Clinical outcomes, including survival and toxicity, were compared between the two groups. Imaging assessments were performed before and 1 month after radiotherapy. The evaluations included the tumor response to induction therapy, treatment-related adverse events, complications during and after CCRT, tumor response after CCRT, progression-free survival (PFS), and overall survival (OS). Follow-up data were collected through regular check-ups or inpatient records. If unavailable, patients were contacted by phone. Results: Compared to the induction chemotherapy group, the objective response rate (ORR) of tumors after induction treatment in the induction immunochemotherapy group was 67.6% (23/34) versus 45.2% (47/104) (P = 0.023). After CCRT, the ORR in two groups was 79.4% (27/34) versus 60.6% (63/104) (P = 0.045). The median followup time was 27.9 months for the induction immunochemotherapy group and 30.3 months for the induction chemotherapy group. The median progression-free survival (PFS) for two groups was 21.3 months versus 13.1 months (P = 0.007), and the median overall survival (OS) was not reached versus 41.7 months (P = 0.031). Treatment-related toxicities of grade 1-2 were 64.7% versus 54.8% (P = 0.311), and grade 3-4 toxicities were 41.2% versus 41.3% (P = 0.986). No grade 5 treatmentrelated toxicities were observed in either group. Conclusions: Induction immunochemotherapy followed by CCRT for patients with LS-SCLC is effective and safe with a high ORR, PFS and OS rate, as well as a tolerable toxicity profile. Larger, randomized controlled trials are required to confirm our findings.

title: Randomized Phase II Trial of Adjuvant Radiochemotherapy, Concurrent Versus Sequential, in Resected pN2 NSCLC. ARCCS Trial
authors: Borghetti P., Sepulcri M., Merlotti A., Bonu M.L., Vagge S., Scotti V. et al.
abstract: Introduction: Postoperative radiotherapy (PORT) in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with mediastinal involvement has been a therapeutic option for a long time. However, recent randomized phase 3 trials (LungART and PORT-C) have shown no benefit in disease-free survival and increased toxicity with PORT. This randomized phase 2 study aimed to compare sequential and concomitant adjuvant chemoradiotherapy using a modern radiotherapy technique. Methods: From 2020 to 2023, NSCLC patients were randomized after surgery to receive cisplatin-based chemotherapy for up to 4 cycles and sequential (Arm A) vs concurrent (Arm B) PORT (50 Gy/25 fractions). Intensitymodulated radiation therapy (IMRT) and image-guided radiation therapy (IGRT) were mandatory. The study had 2 co-primary endpoints: G3+ acute esophageal toxicity and locoregional recurrence-free survival (LRFS). Results: Twenty-four patients were enrolled, 11 in Arm A and 13 in Arm B. The study was closed due to low accrual, mainly due to the COVID-19 pandemic and negative data of LungART and PORT-C trials that discouraged continuation of the study. Patients and disease characteristics are shown in the table. All cases were adenocarcinomas and all patients underwent lobectomy. Nearly 30% of patients had R1 resection due to positive surgical margins or extracapsular nodal extension. In arm B, positive mediastinal nodes were higher and detected ones were lower than in arm A. Sixteen patients completed the planned 4 cycles of chemotherapy, 9 in arm A and 7 in B. One patient in arm A had regional progression after chemotherapy. After a median follow-up of 44 months, 1 local, 2 regional and 15 distant recurrences were recorded, while 17 patients were still alive. The 3-year LRFS was 82% and 91% in arms A and B, respectively. No severe acute esophageal toxicity was ever reported in either arm. Grade 1-2 esophageal toxicity occurred in 2 and 4 patients in arms A and B, respectively. Two patients in arm B reported pneumonitis. Conclusions: The small sample size does not allow definitive conclusions to be drawn on the usefulness of postoperative concom itant radiochemotherapy. However, the use of modern radiotherapy resulted in a reduction of toxicities and an improvement in locoregional control. Recently, the management of pN2 NSCLC has radically changed with the advent of immunotherapy and targeted therapies, confining PORT to an extremely marginal role. Today, in extremely selected cases (high locoregional risk), PORT could still be proposed to reduce the risk of locoregional recurrence, but only by adopting the highest radiotherapy standards.

title: SUNRAY-02: A Phase 3 Study of Olomorasib and Immunotherapy in Stage II-III KRAS G12C-Mutant NSCLC After Definitive Therapy
authors: Nadal E., Chaft J., Garassino M., Gray J., Lee S.-H., Lee J.M. et al.
abstract: Introduction: KRAS mutations are well-characterized oncogenic drivers in multiple solid tumors. KRAS mutations are the most com- mon oncogenic driver in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and KRAS G12C accounts for 40% of KRAS-mutant lung cancer. Olomorasib is a potent and highly selective second-generation inhibitor of KRAS G12C, which delivers >90% sustained target occupancy at low absolute exposures in preclinical models. The phase 1/2 LOXO-RAS-20001 study (NCT04956640) showed that olomorasib alone and in combi- nation with pembrolizumab demonstrated promising preliminary ef- ficacy and a favorable safety profile in patients with metastatic KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC. The safety and efficacy of olomorasib in combi- nation with immunotherapy, allows movement into the curative setting, where immunotherapy is the standard perioperative or consolidation therapy with surgery or radiation, respectively. Methods: SUNRAY-02 (NCT06890598) is a global, multicenter, ran- domized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 3 study that aims to determine the efficacy of olomorasib in combination with standard of care immunotherapy following definitive treatment of stage II-III NSCLC. Eligible participants are at least 18 years old, ECOG PS 0-1, with pathological confirmation of NSCLC (resected stage II-IIIB or unresectable stage III based on AJCC 9 t h edition) with known KRAS G12C mutation (tissue or blood) and PD-L1 expression (0-100%). Key exclusion criteria include a known EGFR mutation or ALK rear- rangement, prior history of NSCLC, pneumonitis/interstitial lung dis- ease requiring steroids, or active autoimmune disease. This study includes two parts: Part A enrolls participants with resected stage IIIIIB NSCLC, who received either neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy without achieving a pathological complete response or received adjuvant chemotherapy; Part B enrolls participants with unresectable stage III NSCLC with no evidence of disease progression following concurrent chemoradiation therapy. In Part A, approximately 400 participants will be randomized in a 1:1 ratio to receive either olomorasib or placebo in combination with pembrolizumab. In Part B, approximately 300 participants will be randomized in a 1:1 ratio to receive either olomorasib or placebo in combination with durvalumab. All participants will receive combination treatment for up to approx- imately 1 year, followed by olomorasib or placebo monotherapy to complete up to 3 years of total treatment. The primary endpoint for Part A is disease-free survival (DFS). Secondary endpoints include overall survival (OS), changes in health-related quality of life (HRQoL), safety, and patient-reported tolerability. The primary endpoint for Part B is progression-free survival (PFS) by blinded independent central review. Secondary endpoints include OS, objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), disease control rate (DCR), time to response (TTR), progression-free survival 2 (PFS2), safety, changes in HRQoL, and patient-reported outcomes.

title: Individualized Neoantigen Therapy With Unusual Radiotherapy Enhancement (iNATURE) a Phase II Randomized Study
authors: Zhang Y., Hu Y.-F., Li R., Dai W., Wang Q., Chen W. et al.
abstract: Introduction: Personalized neoantigen-based vaccines targeting tumor- specific neoantigens are emerging as a promising immunotherapy, with recent publication in Nature reporting 9 cases showing no relapse 40 months after surgery. However, in refractory solid tumors, the effect is limited due to significant tumor burden. We hypothesized that neoantigen peptide vaccines combined with immune modulating precision Critical Lesion Radiotherapy (CLERT) can improve local and distant tumor control. This study aimed to evaluate the safety, feasibility, and early efficacy of this approach in metastatic cancer patients who failed systemic therapy. Methods: This is a phase II randomized study (NCT06314087) focusing on refractory metastatic cancers. Eligibilities included metastatic solid tumors with progression after systemic therapy and 'targetable lesions for CLERT. Neoantigens were selected using an AI-powered immunogenicityprediction platform based on whole exosome sequencing by three independent teams. There were two arms: the control arm with placebo, and the vaccine arm receiving fully personalized peptides, BXP- 001. All patients received CLERT one week before vaccine injection, with 5 prime immunizations and four monthly boosters. Control patients crossed over to the vaccine arm upon disease progression. Immune responses were assessed through T cell and antibody testing. Results: This is a pre-planned interim report. A total of 46 patients have been screened with WES genomic testing of over 20000 genes. On average, 2.626 (95% CI: 2.111-3.142) tumor mutation burden were tested. All patients had immunogenic mutations, with tumor neoantigen burden ranged from 0.756-1.099 (median 0.928), 60% patients were found to have 5+ high-ranking immunogentic neoantigens, confirmed by all 3 teams. Upto March 31, 2025, 11 of these NSCLC patients were enrolled and successfully randomized. Two patients died from tumor progression accociated complication before protocol treatment start. Seven patients are currently undergoing treatment or have already completed it, while others are awaiting treatment initiation. Among the 7 treated patients treated per protocol, 3 and 4 patients were assigned to the neoantigen and control arms, respectively. 3 of 4 control patients had disease progression on placebo arm and were subsequently crossed over to the vaccine arm. All 7 patients received vaccine had disease controlled with a median follow-up of 2.73 months (95% CI 1.16-6.39). The median PFS for the placebo group was 1.4 months (95% CI 1.13-NR), while the median PFS for the neoantigen group has not yet been reached. Immunogenicity analysis of PBMC and plasma samples revealed neoantigen-specific immune response in T cell and antibody in all tested patients. There was no grade 2 above toxicity in either of the arms, 14.3% received vaccine had grade 1 injection site bruise and 14.3% fatigue. Conclusions: This study at the first time demonstrated iRT of fully personalized neoantigen peptide vaccine (BXP-001) and CLERT was feasible, safe, and capable of eliciting neoantigen-specific immune responses in all analyzed patients with refractory solid tumors. The preliminary results in PFS between vaccine and placebo groups appears to be promising. Further study with larger sample size in multicenter setting is beining kicked off to confirm this promising strategy.

title: EA5181: Phase 3 Trial of Concurrent and Consolidative Durvalumab vs Consolidation Durva Alone for Unresectable Stage 3 NSCLC
authors: Varlotto J.M., Xie Y., Pennell N., Oettel K., Lovly C.M., Anagnostou V. et al.
abstract: Introduction: Consolidation durvalumab improves survival for patients (pts) with unresectable stage 3 nonsmall cell lung cancer (NSCLC) after definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CRT). However, the optimal timing of initiation of immune checkpoint inhibition is unknown. EA5181 tested the hypothesis that starting durvalumab concurrent with CRT and continuing as consolidation would improve overall survival compared to consolidation durvalumab alone. Methods: 662 pts with previously untreated, unresectable stage III A C (N = 635) NSCLC or with mediastinal node recurrence after prior surgery (N = 27) and ECOG performance status (ECOG PS) 0-1 were randomized to either concurrent durvalumab with chemotherapy (weekly carboplatin/paclitaxel, cisplatin/pemetrexed x 2 cycles or cisplatin/etoposide x 2 cycles) and 60Gy radiotherapy (Arm A), or to CRT alone (Arm B). Patients in both arms who completed CRT and did not progress, had treatment-related toxicity < grade 1 within 45 days of CRT and did not develop pneumonitis were assigned to one year (12 cycles) of durvalumab (Arm C). Stratification factors include age, sex, stage, and chemotherapy regimen. The primary endpoint was OS in the intention-to-treat population, with secondary endpoints of progression-free survival (PFS), objective response rate (ORR), patterns of failure (local vs. distant), and toxicity. The stratified log-rank test was used to compare OS and PFS, while stratified Cox propor tional hazards models were applied to assess the treatment effect. Two-sided Fisher's exact tests were used to compare ORR and recurrence patterns between the two arms. Results: 335 and 327 pts were randomly assigned to arms A and B with median follow -up of 29.9 months (95% CI: 28.4 - 33.1). 277 pts in arms A (82.7%) and B (84.7%) started Arm C. The most common chemotherapy partner was carboplatin/paclitaxel (553/646=85.6%). Median age was 67.1 with 60.6% men, 88.4% White, 8.3% Black and 94.9% Non-Hispanic. The proportions receiving all treatment cycles in step 1 in Arms A and B were 93.7% and 94.0% respectively. The median OS for Arms A and B was 41.5 vs. 39.4 months, p = 0.83, HR = 1.03 (95% CI: 0.80 - 1.32). Disease progression was reported in 152 (45.4%) Arm A /C pts and 144 (44.0%) Arm B /C pts, and median PFS was 15.5 vs. 16.8 months, respectively, p = 0.65, HR = 1.05 (95% CI: 0.86- 1.29). Multivariable analysis noted that OS and PFS were significantly associated with ECOG PS. Prior thoracic surgery was significantly associated with a better PFS. Adenocarcinoma histology and DLCO > 80% were associated with significantly better OS. In step 1, the overall response rates (CR and PR) were 51.3% (Arm A) and 47.1% (Arm B), p = 0.28. There were no significant differences in grade 3-5 or grade 2-5 cardiac/pulmonary events per arm in steps 1 and 2. Local recurrences were 55.9% and 49.6% in Arms A and B, respectively, p = 0.34. Radiation in-field recurrences were noted in 28.6% (Arm A) and 28.4% (Arm B). Conclusions: Concurrent and consolidation durvalumab with CRT in unresectable Stage III NSCLC did not improve OS compared to consolidation durvalumab alone. There were also no differences in ORR, PFS, failure patterns nor toxicity between arms.

title: Hypofractionated vs Conventional Fractionated Radiotherapy With Concurrent Chemotherapy for LS-SCLC: A Multi-Center Phase III Trial
authors: Bi N., Zhang T., Hu X., Zhang L., Zhang J., Cao J. et al.
abstract: Introduction: No randomized trials have directly compared hypofractionated radiotherapy (HypoRT) with conventional radiotherapy (ConvRT) when delivered concurrently with chemotherapy for pa tients with LS-SCLC. This study evaluates whether a 3-week HypoRT schedule is as effective and safe as a 6-week ConvRT schedule. Methods: This multicenter, open label, randomized phase III trial was conducted across 16 hospitals in China. All patients received 4-6 cy cles of cisplatin/carboplatin-etoposide chemotherapy (CT) every 3 weeks. Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to receive either HypoRT (45 Gy in 15 daily fractions over 3 weeks) or ConvRT (60 Gy in 30 daily fractions over 6 weeks), beginning no later than the third cycle of CT. All patients were treated using intensity-modulated radiotherapy. The primary endpoint was overall survival (OS), defined as time from randomization to death from any cause, and analyzed by modified intention-to-treat (mITT). The sample size was based on testing a non-inferiority margin of HR = 1.09 (corresponding to 2-year OS rates of 50% vs. 47%). The alternative hypothesis assumed a hazard ratio of 0.78 (2-year OS 50% to 58%). A total of 540 patients and at least 324 deaths would provide approximately 85% power to declare non-inferiority at a one-sided alpha of 0.025. Results: Be tween November 2016 and December 2022, 530 patients were ran domized to HypoRT (n=261) or ConvRT (n=269). Due to changes in the treatment landscape, the final analysis was conducted prior to reaching the planned number of events. Baseline characteristics of two arms were well balanced. At a median follow-up of 43.4 months, HypoRT did not meet the non-inferiority threshold compared to ConvRT in OS: median OS was 40.2 months vs. 47.9 months, respec tively (HR = 1.04; 95% CI, 0.81-1.33). The estimated 1-, 2-, 3-, and 5- year OS rates were 86.6%, 68.9%, 53.5%, and 42.4% for HypoRT and 87.2%, 68.0%, 55.5%, and 43.9% for ConvRT, respectively. Median progression-free survival (PFS) was also similar (16.5 months vs. 18.0 months; HR = 1.06; 95% CI, 0.86-1.32, p=0.75). Acute treatmentrelated grade >3 adverse events were significantly lower in the HypoRT arm (48.7% vs 67.7%, p<0.001), primarily due to reduced hematologic toxicity. Notably, fewer patients in the HypoRT arm experienced grade >2 lymphopenia during cCRT (60.5% vs 88.8%, p<0.001), as well as 1 month (34.3% vs 48.7%, p=0.01) and 6 months (14.4% vs 28.7%, p=0.008) after RT. Grade >2 radiation pneumonitis (RP) was also significantly reduced in the HypoRT arm (7.7% vs. 14.5%; p = 0.013). No significant differences were observed in late toxicity profiles. Conclusions: This is the first phase III randomized trial directly comparing HypoRT to ConvRT in LS-SCLC patients in modern RT era. Although the primary analysis was conducted prior to full data maturity, the HypoRT regimen demonstrated numerically similar efficacy and a more favorable safety profile, including lower rates of RP, hematologic toxicity, and lymphopenia. These findings support the clinical adoption of HypoRT. Further investigation of HypoRT in combination with immunotherapy is warranted given its potential immune-sparing benefits.

title: Immunotherapy Plus Chemoradiotherapy for Limited-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Trials
authors: Lee C.C., Lee C.K., Hu C., Soon Y.Y., Hegi-Johnson F., Higgins K.
abstract: Introduction: For patients with limited-stage small cell lung cancer (LS-SCLC) planned for definitive chemoradiotherapy (CRT), the optimal approach of integrating immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) remains uncertain. This study aimed to clarify the overall effect of adding ICIs to CRT in patients with LS-SCLC and evaluate whether this effect varied according to prespecified treatment characteristics. Methods: Various medical databases were searched to identify randomized controlled trials (RCTs) evaluating the addition of ICIs to CRT in patients with LS-SCLC. Primary outcome was overall survival (OS). The overall pooled effect was estimated using a two-stage, fixed-effect meta-analysis approach. Differences in overall effect based on treatment characteristics were analysed using either between-trial or within-trial interactions, depending on the availability of data. Prespecified treatment characteristics included (1) ICI timing (concurrent with CRT plus consolidation versus consolidation alone), (2) platinum agent (carboplatin versus cisplatin), (3) thoracic radiation schedule (twice daily versus once daily) and (4) use of prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI). The risk of bias for individual studies was assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 tool. This study is registered with PROSPERO (CRD420251020544). Results: Four eligible RCTs (n = 1,291 participants) were identified. Three RCTs used consolidation ICI alone, while one RCT employed concurrent ICI with CRT followed by consolidation ICI. The overall risk of bias was assessed as low in two published RCTs, and unclear in two others as the evaluation was limited to the conference abstracts. Overall, the meta-analysis showed no significant OS benefit with the addition of ICIs to CRT (hazard ratio (HR) 0.89, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.76-1.05, P = 0.16, I 2 = 43%). However, prespecified subgroup analyses revealed significant effect modification. The relative effect of incorporating ICIs to CRT on OS was more favorable when used as consolidation alone (HR, 0.78, 95% CI 0.63-0.96) compared to concurrent plus consolidation therapy (HR 1.11, 95% CI 0.85-1.45; interaction P = 0.04). Furthermore, carboplatin-based regimen showed a greater OS benefit (HR, 0.68; 95% CI, 0.51-0.90) compared to cisplatin-based regimen (HR, 1.04; 95% CI, 0.82-1.30; interaction P = 0.02). Thoracic radiation dose schedule (twice daily: HR 0.80, 95% CI 0.59-1.07 versus one daily: HR 0.91, 95% CI 0.74-1.10; interaction P = 0.49) and PCI use (PCI: HR 0.90, 95% CI 0.69-1.17 versus no PCI: HR 0.86, 95% CI 0.68-1.09; interaction P = 0.82) did not significantly modify the overall effect. Conclusions: For patients with LS-SCLC intended for definitive CRT, the optimal integration of ICIs might be most beneficial when two criteria are met: (1) ICIs are administered exclusively as consolidation therapy following completion of CRT, and (2) carboplatin serves as the platinum backbone in CRT. The effect of ICIs was not influenced by the thoracic radiation dose fractionation schedule or the use of PCI. These findings raise hypotheses for optimising treatment strategies in LSSCLC.

title: Toripalimab Consolidation After Chemoradiotherapy in Limited-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Phase II, Randomized Controlled Study
authors: Zhang P., Wang D., He W., Dong Y., Zhang H., Situ Y. et al.
abstract: Introduction: Immune checkpoint inhibitors plus chemotherapy has demonstrated survival benefits in the management of extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (SCLC). The integration of immunotherapy with chemoradiotherapy may improve survival outcomes in limited-stage SCLC. This phase II, randomized controlled study was designed to evaluate the efficacy and safety of toripalimab consolidation versus observation in patients with limited-stage small cell lung cancer (LSSCLC) after concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CCRT). Methods: We enrolled patients with LS-SCLC who had an ECOG performance status of 0-1 and demonstrated no disease progression following CCRT. Eligible patients were randomized 1:1 to either toripalimab consoli- dation (240mg every 3 weeks for up to 6 months) or observation. Randomization was stratified according to disease stage (I or II vs. III) and receipt of prophylactic cranial irradiation (yes vs. no). The pri- mary end point was progression-free survival (PFS). The secondary endpoints included overall survival (OS), duration of response (DOR) and toxicities. Toxicities were graded according to CTCAE v.5.0. Post hoc power analyses included conditional power (CP) and post hoc predictive power of success. Results: Between June 30, 2020, and May 31, 2024, 96 patients were randomized to receive either toripalimab (n=48) or undergo observation (n=48) following CCRT. The median follow-up duration was 27.8 months. The baseline characteristics are well balanced between toripalimab and observation groups. The median PFS was not reached in the toripalimab group, compared with 11.8 months (95% CI, 3.2-20.5 months) in the observation group(HR= 0.55, 95% CI, 0.30-0.98). The median OS was not reached in the toripalimab group, compared with 33.7 months (95% CI, 20.1-47.3 months) in the observation group (HR= 0.44, 95% CI, 0.20-0.96). The incidence of grade 3 or 4 adverse events was 22.9% among patients receiving toripalimab and 27.1% among patients with observation. Adverse events led to toripalimab discontinuation in 8.3% (4/48) of the patients. Grade 3 or 4 pneumonitis or radiation pneumonitis occurred in 4.2% of the patients in toripalimab group and 2.1% of those in observation group. No grade 5 events occurred in both groups. Conclusions: In patients with LS-SCLC, toripalimab consolidation significantly improved OS and PFS with an acceptable safety profile.

title: Locoregional toxicities associated with immunotherapy plus chemoradiation in locally advanced head and neck cancer: A meta-Analysis of phase 3 trials
authors: Srinivasmurthy R., Ta J., Jones D.T., Hattin R., Nanda R.K., Khalid T. et al.
abstract: Background: Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) is the most common malignancy of the head and neck and ranks seventh among all cancers worldwide. Standard chemoradiotherapy (CRT) traditionally includes cisplatin and 5-fluorouracil. The FDA approval of pembrolizumab in 2019 for recurrent/metastatic HNSCC has prompted investigation into immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) for the treatment of locally advanced (LA) HNSCC. As ICIs are integrated into curative-intent regimens, evaluating associated locoregional toxicities is essential. This meta-Analysis assesses the incidence of locoregional adverse events (AEs) associated with adding ICIs to standard CRT in LA HNSCC. Methods: A comprehensive literature search of MEDLINE and EMBASE was conducted through July 10, 2025. Eligible studies included phase III randomized controlled trials (RCTs) evaluating ICIs in LA HNSCC and reporting locoregional AEs. Pooled risk ratios (RRs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated using the Mantel-Haenszel method. Heterogeneity was assessed using Cochran s Qstatistic. A fixed-effects model was applied. Results: Two phase III RCTs were included (KEYNOTE-412, n=796 ; JAVELIN Head and Neck 100, n=692), totaling 1,488 patients. Both trials compared ICIs (avelumab or pembrolizumab) plus CRT versus CRT alone. The incidence of high-grade odynophagia was significantly higher in the ICI group (4.83%) compared to control (2.29% ; RR 2.11 ; 95% CI: 1.20-3.72 ; p=0.010). No statistically significant differences were observed in the rates of other locoregional AEs, including any-grade or high-grade stomatitis, radiation dermatitis, xerostomia, dysphagia, dysgeusia, oropharyngeal pain, tinnitus, or any-grade odynophagia. Conclusion: The addition of ICIs to standard CRT in LA HNSCC appears to have a comparable locoregional toxicity profile to CRT alone, except for an increased risk of high-grade odynophagia. These findings suggest that ICI-based intensification may be safely considered in selected patients, with attention to managing odynophagia-related symptoms during treatment.

title: Evaluation of progression-free survival as a surrogate endpoint for overall survival in small cell lung cancer: a systematic review
authors: Lin G., Ge F., Yang C., Huang Y.
abstract: Background: Patients with small cell lung cancer (SCLC) face a poor prognosis and have limited treatment options, which has prompted the initiation of clinical trials. Overall survival (OS) is the gold-standard endpoint in clinical trials, reflects the benefits patients derive from interventions. Nevertheless, utilizing OS as an endpoint in clinical trials requires large sample sizes and greater financial supports than using progression-free survival (PFS). This study aims to evaluate whether PFS can serve as a surrogate endpoint for OS, thereby conserving human and financial resources in SCLC clinical trials. Methods: We conducted a systematic review of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) from online databases (PubMed, EMBASE, and the Cochrane Central Library) and international conferences up to January 1, 2025. Hazard ratios (HRs) for PFS and OS, as well as PFS and OS rates, were collected. Weighted linear regression analyses were performed to assess the correlation between PFS benefit and OS benefit, with subgroup analyses based on treatment lines and the trial phase. Results: A total of 43 RCTs involving 15,119 patients with SCLC were analyzed, including 28 trials focusing on treatment-naïve patients and 15 trials evaluating non-first-line therapies. PFS exhibited a moderate correlation with OS benefit in treatment-naïve patients, particularly for those receiving first-line immunotherapy. Moreover, the 1-year PFS rate showed a strong association with the 1.5-year OS in first-line trials and 2-year OS in subsequent-line trials. No significant correlation was observed in patients who had received prior treatments. Conclusions: This study supports the utilization of PFS as a surrogate endpoint for OS in clinical trials involving treatment-naïve SCLC patients, especially receiving first-line immunotherapy. This finding can enhance the efficiency of clinical trial design and facilitate the evaluation of novel therapeutic interventions in this challenging patient population.

title: Comparative Efficacy of Short-Course versus Long-Course Radiotherapy with or without Immunotherapy for Neoadjuvant Treatment of Rectal Cancer: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-Analysis
authors: Li L., Meng X., Wu Q., Wei Y., Liang S., Su T.
abstract: Background: The integration of immunotherapy with neoadjuvant radiotherapy for proficient mismatch repair (pMMR) locally advanced rectal cancer (LARC) is a promising strategy. However, the optimal radiotherapeutic backbone, short-course radiotherapy (SCRT) or long-course chemoradiotherapy (LCRT), remains uncertain due to the absence of direct comparative trials. This network meta-analysis (NMA) aimed to compare the efficacy and safety of these two platforms, each with or without immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs). Methods: We systematically searched PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, and Medline from inception to September 20, 2025, for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing neoadjuvant radiotherapy (SCRT or LCRT) combined with ICIs versus radiotherapy alone in non-metastatic rectal adenocarcinoma. A frequentist NMA was performed using random-effects models. The primary outcome was pathological complete response (pCR) based on intention-to-treat analysis. The secondary outcome was the incidence of grade ≥3 treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs). Treatments were ranked using surface under the cumulative ranking curve (SUCRA) probabilities. Findings: Seven RCTs (1,117 patients) evaluating four strategies were included. For pCR, SCRT+ICIs was ranked as the most effective treatment (SUCRA, 99.2%). SCRT+ICIs significantly outperformed SCRT alone (risk ratio [RR], 1.80; 95% CI, 1.26-2.59), LCRT alone (RR, 2.61; 95% CI, 1.58-4.31), and LCRT+ICIs (RR, 2.08; 95% CI, 1.15-3.76). The addition of ICIs to LCRT showed a non-significant increase in pCR versus LCRT alone (RR, 1.25; 95% CI, 0.92-1.72). Regarding safety, adding ICIs to either radiotherapy platform did not significantly increase grade ≥3 TRAEs, and no significant difference was found between the two combination strategies (SCRT+ICIs vs LCRT+ICIs: RR, 0.98; 95% CI, 0.46-2.07). Interpretation: In this NMA, SCRT combined with immunotherapy was associated with significantly higher pCR rates compared to all other strategies, without a significant increase in severe toxicity. These findings position SCRT+ICIs as a highly effective and efficient neoadjuvant approach for pMMR LARC. The results strongly advocate for direct comparison in future phase III trials focused on long-term survival and organ preservation.

title: Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy and PD-1 Inhibition for Head and Neck Cancer Treatment De-escalation (NeoScorch HN)
abstract: Brief SummaryThe NeoScorch HN study is a single institution multisite phase II trial including 3 cohorts of 25 patients each for patients with newly diagnosed locoregionally advanced, histologically confirmed, head and neck cancer eligible for curative-intent treatment, who will receive neo-adjuvant chemoimmunotherapy-based treatment as well as standard of care adjuvant treatment. The three cohorts include three different aspects of surgical de-escalation in head and neck cancer. The first cohort includes human papillomavirus independent (HPV-) squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck. The second cohort includes HPV-associated head and neck cancer with radiographic evidence of extranodal extension in neck lymphadenopathy. The third cohort specifically includes malignancies of the sinonasal cavity and skull base which have a propensity for invasion of the orbit, skull base, and maxilla. Surgical treatment of all three of these cohorts has significant morbidity including swallowing, speech, and vision among others.

title: WOPPPR: Window of Opportunity Phase I and Phase II Trial Combining PD-1 Inhibition and Neoadjuvant Proton or Photon Radiation Therapy in Recurrent Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma
abstract: Brief SummaryThe purpose of the Phase I study is to see if it is safe to use X-ray photon radiation (XRT) and proton radiation (PT) before surgery in patients with recurrent head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (rHNSCC). The purpose of the Phase II study is to see if using XRT or PT before immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) prior to surgery benefits patients with recurrent head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (rHNSCC).Detailed DescriptionIn phase I, cohorts of 3 to 6 patients will be treated with 3 fractions of RT prior to surgery. A de-escalation cohort is planned if toxicity is exceeded in Cohort 1. The types of RT (XRT and PT) will be tested sequentially. The first cohort will include XRT followed by salvage surgery and adjuvant immunotherapy. The RP2D or dose found to be safe will be decided for XRT first. The RP2D for XRT will then be the starting dose cohort for PT to ensure the dose remains safe. The highest dose found to be safe for both types of RT will be used for Phase II. The dose escalation criteria must be met at each dose level including up to 28 days post-surgical resection in order to enroll and treat pts for phase II or de-escalate to the indicated dose level. Phase II is a two arm window of opportunity multi-center study investigating XRT or PT with PD-1 inhibition in recurrent HNSCC. The study will be conducted in two stages with 2 independent experimental arms based on a Simons 2 Stage Design. PT followed by PD-1 inhibition (Arm 1) or XRT followed by PD-1 inhibition (Arm 2) will be independently compared to a historical control group with the primary endpoint of major pathological response (MPR) and a goal of 35% of patients achieving MPR compared to a baseline of 10% with PD-1 alone. Forty total patients with recurrent HNSCC will be screened and randomized to avoid selection bias to either PT or XRT. Based on a Simon Stage 2 Design, nine patients are required for stage I. Stage II will proceed after determination of Stage I MPR.

title: Window Trial of Fluorescently Labeled Nivolumab-IRDye800 (Nivo800) in High Grade Glioma (HGG)
abstract: Brief SummaryHigh-grade gliomas (HGGs) are among the most aggressive and treatment-resistant brain tumors. Immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors like nivolumab has shown promise, but its efficacy remains variable and poorly understood in this patient population. This clinical trial investigates a novel imaging-enabled formulation of nivolumab-IRDye800 (nivo800) which incorporates a near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent dye to enable real-time visualization of drug distribution within tumor tissue.

title: Phase Ib/II Study Of The Combination Of Cemiplimab Plus Imiquimod and Laser Therapy As Neoadjuvant Treatment In Cutaneous Basal Cell Carcinoma
abstract: Brief SummaryBasal cutaneous cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common skin cancer. Early-stage disease is managed with surgery or radiation that cure more than 95% of patients. Surgical excision is the treatment of choice and by far the most convenient and effective means of achieving cure of any invasive BCC, Surgery is rarely contra-indicated even in old, debilitated patients, but in locally advanced tumors surgery has the potential of having functional and cosmetic consequences, due to a big tumor size or difficult locations and it is not uncommon tumors that are borderline for the indication of curative surgery. Patients with high-risk disease who have large primary lesions are usually not amenable to a definitive cure with local intervention and may experience significant morbidity, disfigurement, or functional deficits. In some patients, the tumors recur and progress locally being radiotherapy and Hedgehog inhibition therapy available options. Recently, cemiplimab was the first immunotherapy approved by the FDA and EMEA for locally advanced BCC after Hedgehog pathway inhibition The imiquimod (1-(2-methylpropyl)-1-H-imidazole \[4,5-c\] quinolone-amine) is a synthetic compound capable of activating the cells of the immune system, helping to control viruses, tumors, and intracellular parasites The combination of imiquimod plus anti PD-1 antibody could be synergistic. The main hypoteis is that combining cemiplimab, an immune checkpoint inhibitor targeting PD-1, and local treatment with the Toll-like receptor 7 (TLR7) agonist imiquimod may increase immune response against tumor and result in an increase in the rate of definitive cure y in patients with basal cell carcinoma (BCC) who have a high risk of recurrence after surgery alone or a high risk of morbidity, disfigurement, or functional deficits: to increase the rate of definitive cure. The CEMIQUID study main aims to evaluate: 1. The safety and toxicity of the combination of intravenous cemiplimab plus topical imiquimod (plus fractional laser therapy) as neoadjuvant treatment in patients with high risk and potentially resectable BCC. 2. The antitumoral activity in terms of 3-years rate of relapse-free survival (RFS) according to RECIST 1.1 of intravenous cemiplimab as single therapy or in combination with topical imiquimod plus fractional laser therapy as neoadjuvant treatment in patients with high risk and potentially resectable BCC. Patients with high risk, potentially resectable basal cell carcinoma (BCC), enrolled in the Phase Ib part of the study will receive treatment with intravenous (iv) cemiplimab plus topical imiquimod plus fractional laser therapy as neoadjuvant treatment. Patients enrolled in the Phase II part will be randomized to receive neoadjuvant cemiplimab as a single agent or the Phase Ib combination with imiquimod and fractional laser therapyDetailed Description1. RATIONALE Combining cemiplimab, an immune checkpoint inhibitor targeting PD-1, and local treatment with the Toll-like receptor 7 (TLR7) agonist imiquimod may increase immune response against tumor and result in an increase in the rate of definitive cure y in patients with basal cell carcinoma (BCC) who have a high risk of recurrence after surgery alone or a high risk of morbidity, disfigurement, or functional deficits: to increase the rate of definitive cure. 2. OBJECTIVE Combining cemiplimab, an immune checkpoint inhibitor targeting PD-1, and local treatment with the Toll-like receptor 7 (TLR7) agonist imiquimod may increase immune response against tumor and result in an increase in the rate of definitive cure y in patients with basal cell carcinoma (BCC) who have a high risk of recurrence after surgery alone or a high risk of morbidity, disfigurement, or functional deficits: to increase the rate of definitive cure. Primary objective: * The primary objective for the Phase Ib part of the trial is to evaluate the safety and toxicity of the combination of intravenous cemiplimab plus topical imiquimod (plus fractional laser therapy) as neoadjuvant treatment in patients with high risk and potentially resectable BCC. * The primary objective for the Phase II of the trial it to evaluate the antitumoral activity in terms of 3-years rate of relapse-free survival (RFS) according to RECIST 1.1 of intravenous cemiplimab as single therapy or in combination with topical imiquimod (plus fractional laser therapy) as neoadjuvant treatment in patients with high risk and potentially resectable BCC. Secondary objectives: * To evaluate pathological complete response rate (pCR) according to Immune-Related Pathologic Response Criteria' (irPRC) criteria. * To evaluate the activity in terms of response rate (RECIST 1.1), duration of response, clinical benefit, RFS/PFS and OS. * To evaluate the rate of resectability (downstaging) of BCC after 4 cycles of treatment. * To evaluate safety of the intended treatment regimen based on the frequency and severity of adverse events and Treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) assessed by NCI CTCAE v5.0. * Translational research 3. PRIMARY TRIAL ENDPOINTS * Phase Ib: incidence of adverse events (AE) and treatment-related AEs (TRAEs) assessed by NCI CTCAE v5.0 * Phase II: RFS rate at 3 years, defined as the proportion of patients free of locoregional progression or recurrence, distant metastasis (RECIST v 1.1). 4. SECONDARY TRIAL ENDPOINTS * Pathologic complete response (pCR) * Objective response rate (ORR) * Clinical benefit rate (CBR) * Duration of clinical benefit (DBC) * Relapse-free survival (RFS) * Overall survival (OS) * Adverse events (AE) and treatment-related AEs (TRAEs) * Molecular alterations in biological samples 5. TRIAL DESIGN CEMIQUID is a multi-center, open-label, phase Ib/II, two-cohort study. The study is divided into two phases: Phase Ib: 6 patients will be included and the main objective will be to evaluate the safety and toxicity of the combination of intravenous cemiplimab plus topical imiquimod (plus fractional laser therapy) as neoadjuvant treatment in patients with high risk and potentially resectable BCC. Phase II: If the treatment is deemed feasible on Phase Ib, 12 additional patients will be included to evaluate the antitumoral activity. On treatment clinic visits will occur every 3 weeks ±3 days. Computed tomography (CT), digital photography and lymph node ultrasonography (if applicable) will be used at baseline, prior to the third dose of neoadjuvant cemiplimab and prior to surgery to assess tumor response. Long term follow up of tumor evolution after surgical intervention will adhere to local standard clinical practice. The trial includes the assessment of safety (AEs, comorbidities) throughout the study period at every visit, and an exploratory study to evaluate the prognostic value and correlation between clinical features or molecular alterations. 6. TRIAL POPULATION Patients with high risk, potentially resectable basal cell carcinoma (BCC). High risk is defined as any of the following: 1. at least 1 large lesion still resectable, but with increased risk for cosmetic disfigurement or functional defects by assessment of the enrolling physician. 2. Having basosquamous, infiltrative, sclerosing/morpheaform, micronodular, and BCC with carcinosarcomatous differentiation features. 3. Patients with large (≥ 3 cm in diameter in areas of intermedium risk of recurrence such as forehead, cheek, chin, neck, scalp or ≥ 5 cm for lesions in trunk/extremities) recurrent BCC. 4. Multicentric tumors that would require a cosmetic disfigurement or functional defects. 7. STUDY TREATMENT All patients enrolled in the Phase Ib part of the study will receive treatment with intravenous (iv) cemiplimab 350 mg every 3 weeks plus topical imiquimod 5% cream self-applied once daily 5 days per week, and ablative fractional CO2 laser 10.600nm (±10nm) at 1- or 3-week intervals, for a total of 4 neoadjuvant cycles (12 weeks). The study treatment may be discontinued prematurely in case of unacceptable toxic effects, or disease progression. Patients enrolled in the Phase II part will be randomized to receive neoadjuvant cemiplimab 350 mg every 3 weeks as a single agent or the Phase Ib combination with imiquimod and CO2 laser. 8. ETHICAL CONSIDERATION Cemiplimab is approved and might be considered the standard of care for patients with metastatic or locally advanced BCC who are not candidates for curative surgery. Imiquimod is a topical standard treatment for patients with BCC. Patients with high risk BCC require surgeries that may involve comorbidities or disfigurement. Combining both treatments may have a synergistic activity and induce tumor size reduction easing its resection. The neoadjuvant timings proposed will not substantially delay the surgical intervention. The use of 4 doses of cemiplimab in the neoadjuvant setting has been previously tested in 79 patients with CSCC and resulted in a feasible and safe therapy. The study is designed as Ib because doses recommended for cemiplimab and imiquimod are well justified by previous experience in clinical trials and use under marketed conditions. Given the different mechanisms of action and the type of administration, it is not expected to have overlapping toxicities that may require dose modifications of the initial recommended doses. Therefore, dose escalation is not required and the trial includes the dosing of one or more cohorts of patients at the recommended dose. The potential risks of cemiplimab and imiquimod are well known based on clinical trials and marketing experience. Most frequent AEs are immune-related and could be properly managed with adequate care. The protocol already includes safety measures to minimize the risks. Therefore, the potential benefits of cemiplimab and imiquimod as combined neoadjuvant treatmnt in patients with BCC outweigh the risks. The study will be conducted in accordance with the principles of the Helsinki Declaration Adopted by the 18th World Medical Assembly, Helsinki, Finland, June 1964 updated to its latest version Helsinki, Finland, October 2024. With the Good Clinical Practice (GCP) standards issued by the Working Party on Medicinal Product Efficacy of the European Economic Community (1990) (CPMP / ICH / 135/95).

title: Clinical Study on the Safety and Efficacy of Autologous Immune Cell Therapy for Advanced Solid Tumors
abstract: Brief SummaryThe autologous immune cell induction technology used in this project involves transforming peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC) into autologous DC cells, NK cells, CIK cells and other immune cells through cytokine induction, and then re-administering them to the patients. This therapy utilizes biotechnology to culture the immune cells of cancer patients in vitro and then re-infuse them back into the body, stimulating and enhancing the body's own immune function, killing and inhibiting cancer cells, eliminating small and residual lesions, or achieving the goal of treating cancer by significantly inhibiting the proliferation of residual cancer cells.Detailed DescriptionThis project focuses on the DC autologous immunocyte therapy based on the core technology of screening tumor antigen dominant CTL epitope peptides through independent patent technology. It is a cutting-edge research direction in the field of tumor immunotherapy in recent years. The non-engineered DC cells induced by special induction techniques can effectively overcome the shortcomings of the poor therapeutic effect of engineered gene editing technology on solid tumors, and effectively achieve targeted killing of tumors. The DC-CIK immunocyte therapy adopted in this project utilizes the original discovery of HLA-A molecule-restricted tumor-related antigen CTL epitope peptides to modify DC. On the one hand, through subcutaneous injection in vivo, it induces CD8+ T cells to produce CTL with memory function to play a role in eliminating and monitoring the protein with high expression of the tumor antigen source. On the other hand, it induces CD8+ T cells to produce CTL with memory function in vitro, and directly reinfuses CTL to exert the role of eliminating and monitoring the protein with high expression of the tumor antigen source. In the previous experimental studies, the CTL induced by G22 antigen-loaded DC has shown significant "identification" and targeted "killing" capabilities against various solid tumor cell lines and primary tumor cells isolated from tumor tissues. In animal experiments, it also shows the ability to induce the body to produce a strong immune response against various solid tumors such as breast cancer and non-small cell lung cancer, significantly inhibiting tumor growth, prolonging progression-free survival, and showing no obvious toxic side effects, having a promising application prospect. It has high individualization: customized based on the tumor mutation spectrum of patients, avoiding "off-target" damage to normal tissues. It has high safety: clinical studies have shown that the side effects are mild, mainly including injection site reactions or transient fever 37. Combined treatment potential: it can be used in combination with PD-1 inhibitors, chemotherapy or radiotherapy to enhance efficacy. At present, self-active immune cell therapy based on tumor antigen-specific CTL epitope peptides has achieved significant progress in both research and clinical application in China. Personalized tumor vaccines, targeted immunoregulatory strategies for the tumor microenvironment, and combination treatment strategies are providing new concepts and methods for tumor therapy. In summary, the "Clinical Research on Safety and Effectiveness of Autologous Immune Cell Therapy for Advanced Solid Tumors" is expected to provide a clinical trial basis for the development of safe and effective clinical immunotherapies for patients with solid tumors.

title: A Randomized Phase III Trial of Carboplatin, Paclitaxel, Pembrolizumab Versus Carboplatin, Paclitaxel, Bevacizumab Versus Carboplatin, Paclitaxel, Pembrolizumab, Bevacizumab in the Treatment of pMMR, TP53 Mutated Advanced or Recurrent Endometrial Cancer
abstract: Brief SummaryThis phase III trial compares the effect of bevacizumab in combination with carboplatin, paclitaxel and pembrolizumab to the usual treatments of carboplatin and paclitaxel with or without pembrolizumab in treating patients with stage III, IVA or IVB mismatch repair protein proficient (pMMR) and TP53 mutated endometrial cancer that may have spread from where it first started to nearby tissue, lymph nodes, or distant parts of the body (advanced) or that has come back after a period of improvement (recurrent). Bevacizumab is in a class of medications called antiangiogenic agents. It works by stopping the formation of blood vessels that bring oxygen and nutrients to tumor. This may slow the growth and spread of tumor. Carboplatin is in a class of medications known as platinum-containing compounds. Carboplatin works by killing, stopping or slowing the growth of tumor cells. Paclitaxel is in a class of medications called antimicrotubule agents. It stops tumor cells from growing and dividing and may kill them. Immunotherapy with monoclonal antibodies, such as pembrolizumab, may help the body's immune system attack the tumor, and may interfere with the ability of tumor cells to grow and spread. Adding bevacizumab to the combination of carboplatin, paclitaxel and pembrolizumab may be more effective than the usual treatment combinations of carboplatin and paclitaxel with or without pembrolizumab in treating patients with advanced or recurrent pMMR and TP53 mutated endometrial cancer.Detailed DescriptionPRIMARY OBJECTIVE: I.
